utterances: We need people at the bottom of the economic chain and we need people at the top.

Both - a big chunk at the low end and a good chunk at the top end.
Well, let me just point out the point system as proposed in this compromise Senate bill - I'm not saying it's the perfect system - but it's the one that they're debating right now.
Forty-seven points out of a possible 100 go for employment.
So presumably, if the Labor Department says we need agricultural workers, you can get a lot of points if you're a lettuce picker.
But, the next thing is education, a maximum of 28 points.
So, if you're a lettuce picker with a Ph.D., you're going right to the top of the list.
But the education also includes an apprenticeship and a GED, and the - I mean, I'm not happy.
I'm a free market conservative.
I'm not happy with the Labor Department deciding what kind of sectors of the economy are going to grow.
I mean, that's what brought down the Soviet Union.
But I do think it's important that we are going to need those low-skilled workers.
So if that's the best way they can figure out to get them points then…
What's the other thing that brought down the Soviet Union.
So - right.
So let's, you know, let's say…
Jeanne Butterfield.
…we would agree that we need to tweak the point system that's proposed here because it does need to be sensitive to every level of our economic system.
Right.
But I would also go back to the original point and argue that we need not to abolish the family system…
But we're not.
That these two things could exist alongside of each other in adequate numbers to do a much better job than we do today.
And thanks very much for the call, Virginia.

But the people opposed to this are saying, look, this chain immigration, once you to start letting in one legal resident's cousin and then her cousin, it's just - you lose control, all of the slots then get taken up.
All of the available slots get taken up by people who were relatives as opposed to people who might - you might want to encourage for other reasons.
Right, but let's increase the size of the pie.
And let's…

…segment the pie and protect each of these segments.

And family members will still get points.
I mean, immediate family will still get in and other kinds of family members will still get points…
Sibling only gets four points, though.
It really doesn't cut it, Tamara.

You know, I think that, you know, I think that I have better urging and would urge when this bill goes over to the House, for our elected officials to contemplate keeping from family stream, even of siblings, even of adult children, keep them numerically limited.
Yes, and maybe that means adjusting points so that people get more credit for those relationships.
